channel.title: Careers on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Careers on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Scenes From Unretirement
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about scenes from unretirement.

I did a video recently — and I’ll link it below — about me being unretired And it reminded me that over a year ago I did scenes from my life in game development where I showed a bunch of photos I had of and a few videos of stuff I had that happened to me while I was had a game development career Occurred to me. I’m back into game development. I should continue that video, so that’s what this is. I’m going to go away and just show you images and some videos and talk over them, and I’ll be back at the end, All righty. So let’s start with. I arrived back at Obsidian, here I am outside the offices. This is the first day I got back, October 30th 2025..

item.title: Catalyst Team Members
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about catalyst team members.

And by catalyst I’m not talking about the people whose life essences I absorb in order to stay youthful — Oh shoot, it’s not working anymore. No, I’m talking about people on the team that just seem to make the overall team better just by being on the team. And sometimes it’s really hard to identify how they’re doing it I eventually started calling these team members catalysts, because they’re like chemical catalysts that make reactions go faster or more efficiently or happen more readily at lower temperatures, but don’t themselves get used up in it.

item.title: Game Development As An Identity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game development as an identity And

this is probably going to be a fun Friday video because it’s going to be about game development, but it’s going to be kind of personal, Not 100% sure where it’s going to go, maybe a little rambly, Sorry about that. So 5 years ago — right now it’s January of 2026.. Sorry, there’s a fire truck outside. That’s interesting, so it’s January of 2026, and back in May of 2020, so over five years ago, I semi-retired And what that means is I went from being an employee at Obsidian to being a contractor And I consulted at other companies too — all part-time. It wasn’t what I was doing full-time. This let me see a lot of different game development processes. It was fascinating to do it I actually got to work on a lot of toys. I have two videos on semi-retirement, One talking about semi-retirement and the other one talking about why I even did it The whole time.

item.title: It's OK To Be Proud
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why it’s okay for you to be proud, especially of your work and things you do.

And this is probably going to be a bit of a meandering video, so bear with me, but I think it deserves being said, especially in this day and age and on the internet and stuff like that. But I’ll get to it, if you’ve watched this channel for even a tiny amount of time, you will know that I tell a lot of stories. I have a lot of stories to tell. I’ve been doing this for a long time.

item.title: Game Developer Stage Limitations
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game developer stage limitations, which is a somewhat complicated talk.

And yes, it’s dark because in Seattle, once summer ends, it starts getting darker earlier in the evening and it stays dark later in the morning And I get up around. You know, I’m up at this desk by 600..

Sun’s not coming up for another half hour. I’m not waiting for it. This topic on the limitations by game production stage is complicated. It wasn’t something I would have talked about a year or two ago because I had to lay a whole foundation. So I’m going to reference videos while I talk, Recommend you watch the ones I referenced, or at least have watched them, because I’m not going to redefine terms and get lost in talking about game development roles. But first I do want to talk about what I’m not talking about here, because I’ve had. I’ve talked about limitations before. I’ve talked about my own limitations. Um, I have.

item.title: My Uncredited Game
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my uncredited game.

I’ve mentioned it obliquely here and there that there’s a game out there that you know I developed that does not have my name in the credits by my choice. So it’s a fun Friday. I thought I’d talk about it, talk about why I had my name removed, and it will be a story. So let me explain everything that happened. So this is back in the ‘9s and a company had — I’m going to keep the company out of — this Company had a shipped game. So they had a game that they already designed, coded, tested and shipped. I had nothing to do with it and they had done two patches on it, one pretty extensive.

item.title: Why People Quit
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why people quit.

I recently did a video called eating your own dog food — about if you make a product, in particular, a game, you should use it, You should play it, You should eat your own dog food. In the comment section, a lot of people were asking about people who don’t eat their own dog food and, more particularly, why those people would even be in the industry or stay in the game industry if they didn’t play games. And I thought I’d address that in this video, like drill down on it, because I’ve been in the industry.

item.title: Recognizing Good Coworkers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about recognizing good co-workers — and by good I mean good for working with, for you.

And I wanted to talk about this because making games is very stressful. I’m sure that’s come out in my videos and you see all kinds of behaviors from people when they’re stressed. And I wanted to talk about that because, if you’re lucky, you can find some people that you really love working with and that together you make really good games with, and you can stick with those people for decades I have. Now, when people get stressed, there’s some people that fall into one of two camps: either they just start saying yes to everything, they’re just they’re yes, men, that’s a great idea, Tim. That’s a great idea. Let’s do it, and they’re doing this because they just want to avoid argument. They just tell me what to do, I’ll do it.

item.title: Showcasing Your Work
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about showcasing your work.

I’ve talked on this channel about making games, making demos for your games, making game design portfolios to try to get a job, but I’ve never really talked about how, exactly what they you, how you should put them together and what you should decide on to show in them. In fact, sh kovich Dev, during my game design portfolio video, left this comment: this is a good video, but I think what most people struggle with is how to effectively showcase all those things that you laid out. Would you do a video about it?

item.title: Mentoring
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about mentoring.

Request for me to Mentor someone is probably the most frequent topic of emails and DMs. I get about this Channel and I turn them all down and I will tell you why later. But I totally understand the impulse to ask someone to give you advice, uh, to warn you of some of the pitfalls coming up, and I get it I I mentioned, I’ve mentioned several times on this channel that I wished IID had a mentor, both professionally and personally. When I was younger I really could have benefited from having someone who was older and wiser and more experienced to just tell me how some things work in life, in business, in game development. It all would have helped me a great deal and, interestingly, I think I was open to it I look back at my early projects and how they would have been better and I, toally, would have been open for someone to give me that advice. But my first project, when I worked at Pegasus software when I was 16 years old, later turned into cybron. I was 16 and everyone else who worked there — there were a few people in their 20s, most people were in their 30s and I think there was even one or two people in their 40s.

item.title: Insecurity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about insecurity.

This going to be an interesting video because it may be a little rambly, but it’s something I’ve been thinking about for almost a year and let me let me tell you why. So I’ve talked on this channel on many different videos about why people have said they didn’t really like working with me. Um, I’ve heard back now a lot of people may not like working with me, but they didn’t tell me directly. I’ve heard this from people who ended up liking working with me but understood why others didn’t, or may have talked to people who didn’t. The two biggest ones that I’ve heard were that I get very passionate about my ideas and then, in doing so, other people’s ideas get dismissed. Whether I’m intending to do that or not, it doesn’t feel good when your idea gets brushed aside because I’m, you know, all excitedly talking about my idea and then, related to that, I tended to get more attenion, whether it was in a brainstorming meeting or in any other kind of meeting, or even when press would come in, I’d get excited, I’d want to talk about stuff, so I’d get more attention and people just got frustrated and I can see that I get really into stuff.

item.title: Game Design Portfolio
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about your game design portfolio or, more specifically, what you, as a game designer, should include in a portfolio that you’re sending to a game company when you apply for a job.

This is me answering gamedev Tommy, who asked for — quote advice on what a game design portfolio should really consist of and how it should present, be presented. For someone who has not worked in a mainstream Studio yet, unquote — good question, um, so let me talk about exactly what I want to see. I’m going to divide it into what I expect to see and what I’d really like to see. So the expect — the expectation — part is: you really should include this. The I like part is: if you got this, it’s what I want to see, and it’ll move you to the top of the pile. Before you start, though, you should probably.

item.title: Remote Work
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about remote work and specifically, this is an answer to a question from R3 M1 who asks: hey Tim, what’s your take on remote staff versus having the team work in an office?

Do you have a preference? What are the pros and cons, Etc. Good question.

I’m really glad the way you phrase it because, like everything, I think remote work has Pros and cons. Um, I don’t think there’s very little out there that I think is 100% good or 100% bad, and maybe that’s my programmer brain thinking, because there’s no perfect sorting algorithm. It all depends on what you’re sorting and how many, and how frequently it changes, and whether it’s already sorted when you go to Resort.

item.title: Technology Changes Jobs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how technology changes jobs.

Now this is going to be kind of a companion video to a couple videos I did before and they’re linked below. It’s called Uh — industry changes and artificial intelligence because it’s sort of an extension to those, and I’m going to refer to some stories I tell in those uh, so you may want to watch them, so you understand the references I give in this video because I don’t want to be too repetitive. I know I’ve been told I’m repetitive repeatedly but I’m trying to avoid that.

item.title: Awards
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Awards, specifically game awards, more specifically, game awards that my games have won and a few that I personally have won.

And then just one thing that’s always eluded me for the most part, with one exception. I want to just talk about it because some of you may be going: oh I, I know how you feel when I, when you get to the end of this, may be going, wow, how narcissistic and egotistical are you.

item.title: Teaching
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about teaching, in terms of what lessons I learned by teaching and kind of how I applied some of those lessons to game development.

I’ve mentioned that I was a grad in graduate school for four years, four and a half um from late 87 to Mid 91, and during the time as a graduate student, uh, I was at University of California, Irvine UCI, their school of information and computer science. During those four years I had to be a teaching assistant — it’s they will pay you to be a TA and I ta a lot of classes. I te programming languages, uh, basic. I ta te um operating systems and that’s one I actually really liked. I operating systems through three times because I really like the professor, uh, Steven Franklin, and it was taught at 8:00 in the morning and it was an optional class, so it was full of students who really wanted to be there because they didn’t need to be and it was 8. In the morning so it kind of weeded out people who didn’t want to learn about operating systems.

item.title: Hiring People
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about hiring people.

This is come up a lot. I’ve had a video half prepared for this for a long time. I’ve noticed people here and then when I talk about Staffing up a project or interview questions they would. They would ask me: well, how did you hire people, how did you go about hiring people? So I want to talk about how and when I did that, because it’s a little surprising how long it was before I ever had to do that for all my beginning games. I was simply placed in them, I was assigned to it. So, Grand Slam Bridge, BS tail construction set, Rags to Riches, I was just assigned.

item.title: Are You Above Average?
item.description: Hi everyone. It’s me Tim. Are you above average? Let’s talk about that today. I want to talk about whether or not you think you are above average at something like: are you good at, really really good at — this thing better than most other people? So let’s set aside impostor syndrome — I have a whole video on that. I’m not talking about that — those times where you feel like, oh no, someone’s GNA find out that I’m terrible and fire me.

item.title: Game Development Roles
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game development roles.

Now I could have sworn I had talked about this before, but the closest I got was programming — programming roles. But I have talked a lot about what makes a good game director, traits I look for in producers and challenges that face, or challenges that programmers, artists or designers have to face. So there’s a lot of related videos and I’ve linked them all in the description, but I’m going to do this one now. I’m answering Seth, the guy GD, who asks: I was wondering if you have a video explaining the job in game development process and what they do: artists, programmers, producers, leads, designers and all that. I was having questions about that. I know that every studio is different. Could you explain it to me? I know it might be silly, their names are self-explanatory but would mean a lot.

item.title: My Worst Job Interview
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my worst job interview.

Now I’ve done videos where I’ve talked about job interviews from the hiring side, about questions I like to ask and tests I like to give. I’ve even talked about questions I’ve received while looking for a job and those videos are linked below. But today I want to talk about the absolute worst experience I ever had looking for a job, and this came out about because I was talking with colleagues in the industry about their worst job interviews and these horrible ones came up about, you know, sexist remarks and really long tests that were work for the project they were interviewing for — just bad questions. And it reminded me that I usually told one job interview story that happened just a few years ago, but I but I realized I had another really awful one that happened near the beginning of my career. So I’m tell you both of them.

item.title: Fallout's Effect On Me
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fallout’s effect on me, which seems like a very personal, self-reflected, reflective, almost narcissistic topic.

But I’m answering someone’s question. Super duper asks: did you ever feel like sometimes your career was sort of defined by Fallout, like obviously it was your what we would call, in the film industry, breakout role, with it being such a massively successful and popular game? But do you ever feel like being tied to Fallout held you back or gave you opportunities you otherwise would not have had, and just for funsies, with it being such an impactful and influential game? Would you care toine on a world wherein Fallout had been cancelled and you think they might have, and how you think that might have affected our culture?

item.title: Lessons By Company
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what lessons I learned by company.

I’ve, in my entire 43 years now, I’ve only worked at five companies, um as an employee — and one of them was my own, and I’ve already done a couple videos on Lessons Learned. The two biggest ones were the biggest lesson I ever learned, which, just to you know, if you didn’t watch it’s that people lie and a lot of people will. Of people lie and a lot of people will lie right to your face. You going to go watch that video.

item.title: Game Development Tunnel Vision
item.description: Sal SE, Tim, par gotcha. I bet you right now some of you are reaching for that YouTube translate button and some of you were thinking, whoa, Tim’s French accent is terrible, trust me, that’s been said. No, I’m doing this video about game development, tunnel vision, because it’s one of the things that occurred to me. I get a lot of questions about a lot of things and early on, when there were less of you, I would answer and say, oh, that’s not something I know or I can’t talk about it I did do a video on things I can’t or won’t answer, but since then there’s been a lot more questions that Encompass a lot more things, even about game development or tangentially related game development, that I literally cannot answer. I either don’t know it or I’m not qualified to answer.

item.title: Selling A Game Design Spec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about selling a game design spec.

This is actually from a question I got from Nash kruka, I hope, kka. I’m hope I’m pronouncing that right. Um, I’ll say Nash from now on, because it looks like you may have changed your name on the account since when you asked this question and when I’m answering it. But Nash asked: if someone had the entire Wiki for a game like Fallout New Vegas and approached a studio, would it even mean anything to them? Ideas are cheap, but what is a fully thought out game iname Universe worth?

item.title: Would I Work On Fallout Again?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer the question: would I work on Fallout again?

This: in the last few months this question has overtaken my most frequently Asked question — how do I get a job in the game industry — which I have made a video with an answer and then a video about the answer. So I’ve done — how to get a job in the game industry — and then making a demo which is the answer to that. So I want to answer. What I work on followed again, but I have to realize, you have to realize the answer is complicated, but not because of what you think. First of all, there’s a lot of different questions being asked. There.

item.title: So You Want To Make Games?
item.description:
It’s me, Tim, so you want to make games today?

I want to answer a question from zaching Tut who asks: I was wondering if you could do a video about things to consider if you’re thinking about getting into the gaming industry, H? Um, this is kind of a big question. Uh, the first thing I want to do is say that I’m to answer this question is really me asking you a bunch of questions. It’s a lot of questions that only you, each individual watching this, can answer. I’m going to break it down into two categories. First, I think you need to ask yourself if you even want to go into the game industry.

item.title: Being Underestimated
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about being underestimated.

This is kind of the opposite of impostor syndrome. Did a whole video about It’s linked below: um, impostor syndrome is when you feel like you’re not good enough for you know, for whatever you’re trying to do, and I’ve talked about that and I’ve talked about I’ve have felt imposter syndrome. I felt it way further into my career than a lot of people probably thought.

item.title: Why I Directed One Last Time
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I directed one last time and specifically what I mean by that is why I stepped up to be a game director on The Outer Worlds, even after I said I wi it.

This is to answer a question from zombified gamer series who asks: I’m curious on what exactly happened that allowed you to step up and direct the The Outer Worlds. You mentioned that something happened and healed you over time. I’m genuinely curious if you could tell what that story on how you overcome, overcame the demoralization that you went through.

item.title: Snapshots Through My Career
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I wanted to do something fun that I’m going to call snapshots through my career, and

this is kind of this kind of came up because I’ve done a few videos already: one where I showed scenes of a life in game development, which was a lot of pictures that mostly I took during game development — um, except for all the ones of that people took of me at my desk — and I’ve also done a video on data about my games and I’ll link those below, but they’re not very personal. So what I wanted to do was show you me at every stage of my career — I guess I’d call it like every game I made. So what I did is I went through all the photos — I have thousands of them — and I found a photo of me taken during the development of each one of my games. So I’m going to talk about the game and the year it came out and then show a picture of me at that. Age, so I thought it would be fun. It’s nothing huge, so feel free to skip it, but people seem to like the other ones so I thought I’d do this one.

item.title: Game Development Contracting
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game development Contracting.

This is to answer a question from Guerilla suplex who asks: what is it like Consulting for companies? Also, how involved are you with the development process when Consulting?

Good question, um, just to give a summary, up until July of 2020, I was an employee at obsidian. I had moved up to Seattle intending to remain an employee, but when I got up there they said: oh, we can’t do that. Uh, they weren’t set up yet to do to have employees out of state, so I switched to Contracting. This was not the first time I had contracted, um. If you watched my career summary video, I mentioned when I was at cybron — Pegasus was originally called and then it changed it same to cybron — when I was a teenager and in college, that was all part-time work during Summers and breaks and it was pretty much Contracting. And then when I started at interplay in 1991, I was a contractor. So I made barale construction, set for them under a 14-week contract and then when that ended, they hired me full-time in January of 92. So pretty much since ‘92 I’ve been an employee until 2020. Uh, I’m, 2020 was a weird Year all around, but so what I want to do, like I often do, is I will talk about pros and cons of Contracting so you can decide for yourself whether it’s better or worse than being an employ, being an employe of a company. Um, because it’s really up to you what kind of person you are and what risks you want to take and how good you are at uh, finding clients. So first I’ll do the pros of being a contractor, uh, first, you and — and I I mentioned this, uh, just to start off — don’t accept an exclusive contract. Um, most places won’t do this, but if someone does, say they want you as a contractor and they want you to be exclusive — meaning you can’t work anywhere else you — and you need to decide whether you want to do that — um, I would definitely ask for more money.

item.title: Jobs, Careers, And Callings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about having a job versus a career, versus a calling.

Now you may already know what these three different things are, but I’m going to Define them and I also want to talk about how, over the last 40 some years, I’ve watched the proportion of people in each of these three categories change over time in the game industry. Um, it’s something I’ve seen people make comments sometimes going: well, it’s just a job. You know you’re getting taken advantage of at your job, and I want to just point out that some people don’t just view this as a job, and it’s not a small number of people.

item.title: Making A Demo
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about making a demo.

I’ve probably said that phrase in so many other videos, but I’m constantly asked. Even now, it’s been over a year since I’ve been doing this Channel and I’m still asked: hey, Tim, how can I get a job in the industry, despite there being a video titled how to get a go job in the game industry, which I’ll link below. In fact, all the videos I mentioned I’m going to link below, and I always answer this same thing: make a demo. Um, Tim, should I go to school? Make a demo?

item.title: Industry Changes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about industry changes because there have been a lot of stuff going on: a lot of layoffs recently, a lot of concern about AI taking jobs.

People are freaking out and let me just make this clear at the beginning, so no one takes this all out of context later, even though I know some people are. It is bad to lose your job. I’ve lost my job, uh, in the past. It’s bad when you lose your job. That is not what I’m talking about here. I’m talking about news and YouTube, pundits and, you know, industry folks saying the industry is collapsing, it’s collapsing, it’s, horrible.

item.title: Career Advice From Adam Savage
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about some career advice from Adam Savage.

Yes, MythBusters, Adam Savage. I’m a huge MythBusters fan and, because I have a YouTube subscription, I’ve ended up watching quite a number of YouTube channels, and one of them is Adam Savage’s tested by the way, it’s a really good Channel. You should watch it. Uh, this particular video I’m making — after I just watched quit taking it personally — which I’ll link below, which is one of several pieces of advice that Adam Savage gave on your career. Basically, he gave career advice to people who were, uh, sending in questions, and I wanted to talk about it because, as I watch this video, I was like: oh yeah, that’s exactly from my own career.

item.title: Perseverance
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about perseverance.

Specifically, I want to talk about all the speed bumps I had in my career, all the times I thought about leaving the industry and where I think many of you probably would have left, and I wanted to make this positive because I wanted to point out I did persevere. But several times in on this channel, um, people have said, hey, you, had a great career and I’m like there was some speed bumps. And also recently I talked to someone who’d been in the industry as long as I have and we were both amazed that there were always times where it’s like, well, I guess this is the end, I guess this is where my career ends, and then it didn’t. So I’m going to go over some times where I think that happened and just talk about, like, what was going on in my head. So my first job was at Pegasus software, which changed it name to cybron, making Grand Slam.

item.title: Remembering The Game Developers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the people in game development and specifically

, this is sort of an answer to a question that Bruce schlick bur asked on the channel. I worked at interplay at the same time as Bruce. We worked on different projects but we knew each other.

Um, he asked at the end of the day, is it about the process or the end result for you? And I said I replied to him in the channel and said, oh, it’s the process, it’s definitely the process. Um, there are early interviews with me in the ’90s and early 2000s where, when I was asked who my games were for, I would just be honest and go: we make these games.

item.title: Job Interview Tests
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about job interview tests.

I’ve had a lot of requests to talk about this and I know people hate them. People have always stated all these reasons they hate them. I get it, but let me tell you why. In the middle of a sit down in-person job interview, we ask you questions and give you what the whole. I’ll go over what the whole range of tests are, from just asking you a question that you verbally respond to asking you to stand in front of a whiteboard and sketch out your answer. First of all, why do we do this?

item.title: Being Proactive
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about being proactive.

I talked about this about a month ago in a video called good team characteristics, where I talked about five characteristics I really liked in people that I enjoyed working with, and one of them was being proactive, and by that I meant if you see something that needs fixing, at the very minimum, ask about it. You can also offer to fix it. If you know how to fix it, perhaps you even do fix it, and I’ll talk about that in a minute, about fixing something that you see is broke: broken. As soon as you see it being broken, talk about when it’s a good idea to do that before you even ask anybody about it. But I wanted to talk about this because it was really really an important characteristic — and I see some people wrap that up in the whole passion argument about: oh, people think it’s part of passion and passion is bad because it makes you used by the corporation and whatever reasons people have for not liking their job or doing a lot of work on it or being lazy or whatever. Whatever your reason is for why you don’t like passion — great, this is something different.

item.title: Scenes From A Life Of Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to show you scenes from a life spent in game development.

Let me tell you where this came from. I’m thinking, so do not hold me to this — I’m thinking about writing a book on game development. Basically, get rid of all the Memoir stuff, that gets people riled up and you know why don’t you give me credit, why did you tell the story about me? BL, just stuff about here’s how I recommend making games.

item.title: Sometimes It's Just Business
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about that phrase — nothing personal, it’s just business — which is usually used by companies when they’re about to do something not too nice and they don’t want you to take it personally.

It’s just business. I want to tell some stories about friends trying to use me to get a job or getting a job through me, where it didn’t end up well, because the moral of the story is it really wasn’t personal, it really was business. Now, if you have a job in the game industry, I guarantee you probably have stories like this. A lot of people try to get in the industry through their friends or family members. I’m not even going to talk about the number of times I’ve been contacted by relative to either get them a job or one of their kids a job, or even how a woman I went to high school with 30 years ago contacted me once to get her son a job.

item.title: Good Team Characteristics
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about people I like working with, and

this is an interesting question from postal 0311, who asks: could you please tell us about the characteristics of the people you enjoyed working with, the characteristics of the people you felt helped you achieve the most? Basically, what can we do and act to be better team members in a project? That is a great, question.

item.title: Job Interview Questions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about job interview questions.

I did a video called interview questions. I’ll link it below, but that was about press interview questions but a lot of people assumed is going to be about job interviews and asked for this video. So let me talk about questions I like to ask in job interviews and questions I’ve been asked and just job interviews in general. So I’ve been involved in many job interviews on both sides of the table. When I go to job interviews now where I’m hiring someone, I assume people there are going to ask design and code questions for them to work through.

item.title: My Work Pattern For Designing
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my work pattern for Designing, and you can consider this a companion video to the one I did on programming, which I’ll put a link in the description.

This is actually answering a question from post nuke Productions, who asks: you’ve done a few videos about what it’s like to program a game, so could you give us an idea of what a day working as a designer is like? I like this question, um, I’ll clarify it, though that I’m going to write down what I assume it’s like. Being a system designer. A level designer and a narrative designer would have a very different day. Level designers would work with artists more. Uh, narrative designers would be more interested in the quests and the storyline and spend a lot of their Day writing characters. So I’m going to go as if it’s a system mechanic designer, um, because that’s what I’m most familiar with. By the way, you might want to check out the work pattern. For programming, because a lot of things I’m going to mention here are very similar to what I do when I’m a programmer. It’s not that I view the same jobs as similar, it’s just when I’m, it’s just me how I do a job.

item.title: School vs. Self-Taught
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the pros and SC cons of going to school versus being self-taught.

If you want to go in the game industry — and remember, this is from my point of view of going to school to learn programming, but this probably has equal measure for Art and Design and other roles that you want to do in games. So let me talk about for a second. Yesterday I talked about a university class that I taught because I I love being in school and I’ve said that I really liked being at UCI, so it was nice to come back and give a little back. Uh, a few weeks before that I talked about how my love for school and all the skills that I think I learned while in school — whether from school or just people — I went to school with the skills that I learned to make games. So this is kind of on top of those.

item.title: Programmer Roles Explained
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer another question, this one from ATA 94, who asks: can you tell me what is the difference between a lead position and a so-called not lead position?

Sure, I can do that now. This is something that has changed a bit over time, but I’d say it’s been pretty stable like this for about the last 20 years and it’s very similar for artists and designers and people in The Sound department and production. So I’m going to describe it for the one I’m most familiar with, which is programmer, and I’ll try to get very detailed with what it is, and then you’ll see how that Maps over to the other departments. I’m going to start at the top with the lead, and I’ll go down to all the various subcategories. So let’s start with the lead programmer. The lead programmer is the person who’s in charge of all the programmers who are working on one particular game. He answers to the game.

item.title: School As Preparation For Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about school as preparation for game development.

Now I’ve touched on this in many videos. I liked being in school.

I know some of you don’t. I know some of you think college is a waste of time and for some of you it would be. For some of you it will not be.

So let me just talk about how School and when I started, even in high school, was sort of a preparation for my whole game Development Career. So let’s start in high school. As I mentioned, is where I first learned about DND and I started playing regular campaigns with my D and D friends. True, I did hear about DN D and play for the first time with naval officers from my mom’s office, but my regular DND experiences started in high school and it wasn’t restricted to just the weekends when we would play. We would spend the whole week talking about what we had done, what we wanted to do house. Rules, things we’d Chang in the game. It was really exciting because there was nothing like it before. DN D was a brand new thing.

item.title: The Decline Of Generalists
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the decline of generalists, and I’ll describe what that is.

But I’ve gotten a lot of questions from people who say what’s changed the most in development over the last 40 years. And I’ve thought about this a lot because there’s so much that’s changed. I mean the nature of games have changed, the Hardware’s changed, the structure of companies have changed, the money involved has massively changed, the diversity in the industry has changed, the just pretty much everything about the industry is wildly different. So this is something that’s very peculiar and specific to the game industry that a lot of people outside the game industry would probably have no ideas even happening. So I’ll have to talk about what I mean by generalist — why it’s, why they’re in Decline and the problems I think that introduces. So first of all, what do I mean by generalist, a generalist is someone who’s skilled in multiple disciplines.

item.title: My Bad Game Development Skills
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game development tasks that I’m bad at.

So I thought I’d talk about those game development skills that have eluded me over many decades and that I finally resolved myself to. You’re not going to learn these, you’re not going to get better at these. It’s better to let someone else do it.

So let me talk about that, so, first of all, I think the biggest thing when I look back at my games, it’s adding or editing, pairing away features. If there’s one problem I always have is I want too many system mechanics in one game. I just when I start thinking it’s like I want this, one makes sense, this, I want this, one makes sense, but only if you have this one to balance it. We need more of this. There’s hey, I’m worried about this kind of character being made and not being supported, so we need to add this, and this in every one of my games. It adds up to too many system mechanics. I think Arcanum was probably the ultimate expression of that, where if there was an idea that anybody had, it went into Arcanum and Arcanum just had everything. I mean it had.

item.title: Why I'm Semi-Retired
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to answer a question from banefane who asks: are you planning to retire from game development?

I don’t mean quite like — quote - I don’t play games anymore, or I don’t talk about games anymore, or I don’t code for fun anymore. Occasionally, unquote. I’m more like quote. This episode of my life is over and for forever and I have no interest in being a serious part of it.

item.title: Career Summary 1981-2023
item.description: Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer a question from asdfjk1227 who asks: can you do a resume video where you talk about every gaming project you did and then a couple, two short versions of interesting events or something, so we can get a better understanding of your experiences in one spot, like 67, 60 seconds per project or company maximum to get a high level?

item.title: My Memoirs
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to revisit something I mentioned in a previous video, where I talked about my notes, and I’ll link that below.

I want to talk specifically about my book, this big, thick hardback I wrote during the pandemic: I — it’s basically my career. I don’t know if I call it my autobiography or my Memoirs, not exactly sure what the difference is between those. But after moving up to Seattle and not knowing anybody — and it was the middle of the pandemic and everything was shut down — I decided to collect all my notes, whether they were physical or digital. I digitized a lot of the physical ones and then I started organizing them by company and, inside, by that, by product. To give you an idea — and I think I’ve mentioned this somewhere else — um, my Fallout notes alone, digitally, or 86 pages long, and that’s not a page per note. That’s the notes, basically: sentence, paragraph, sentence.

item.title: Why I Won't Run Another Business
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer another question, this time from user Delta pi 8859, who asks a large number of questions, luckily about one topic.

Let me ask the questions first. What was the reason why you didn’t create a second Studio after torika games. Do you think running a business is not fun? Did it take the fun out of development? Was quality of life not as expected when doing your own company? Or to defeat the motivation of opening one in the first place because unexpected experiences? Where are the financial considerations?

item.title: Being A "Celebrity" Game Developer
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what it’s like to work with other people who know who I am.

This is actually a funny question. I will read the final question, there was a long Preamble, but I read you the question I got on the channel: do you find a lot more respect in your projects these days, or do people go? Who are you when working with your gaming celeb ass?

item.title: Career Longevity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about career longevity.

I’ve had several questions from people, and not just on the channel. Um, people have asked me this before: how did you last so long in the game industry? And some of these questions have come from people who tried it, who’ve been in the industry and made it two years, five years, 10 years, and then Tapped Out. And I’ve said it once and I say it again: making games is hard. People think it’s fun and you know, uh, you know a silly, you know industry, you know, and oh, there’s a lot harder jobs than that, true, but it’s still a hard job. I think there’s a number of ways I can answer this question. Maybe any one of them’s right, but taken all together, I think it’s why I stayed in the business so long.

item.title: My Work Pattern For Programming
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my work pattern while coding.

This is something several people have asked, um, the one I wrote down as a reminder to do this video. Uh, what have you asked? What kind of work pattern do you personally find most productive when coding? Do you set out designated time periods where you focus entirely on work, then take a break? So let me talk about that. I’ve done a lot of different things when I have worked. I’ve talked about started as a programmer, then started doing a lot of design as well, then got into production, pretty much done everything but art. So, but this talk I’m really going to talk about programming, because I found that of all the tasks I’ve ever worked on in game development, programming is the one that is most sensitive to being interrupted when I’m coding. And I’m sure a lot of programs are like this: you’re kind of building a structure in your head of whatever algorithm you’re working on, what’s left to do on it, what your variables are, what globals you’re thinking about, what other functions need to be written. In fact, a habit I started doing early on is whenever I would get interrupted I had a little keystroke I would do. It was slash, zzz, so it was a comment with three Z’s which just meant I got interrupted at this point.

item.title: Impostor Syndrome
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about insecurity, or what I mentioned in a previous video about game conferences, that feeling of imposter syndrome.

If you don’t know what this is, you’re lucky. It’s that general feeling you get that you can’t do your job, you can’t do it right, you can’t do it well, you can’t get it done in time and eventually you’re going to be found out for the fraud. You are, but relax. Everybody’s felt this way at one time or another. I felt that way occasionally. I still do feel that way. I pretty much felt like that way for most of the time, for about the first, I want to say 15 years. I mean, I don’t want to make anyone concerned, I don’t want to discourage you, but I’d say, until the development of Fallout, I always felt like, oh, I don’t know if I’m, if I belong here, I don’t know if I’m good enough to be in this group and keep.

item.title: Ageism In The Game Industry
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about ageism in the game industry and that’s why I’m wearing a shirt which you may have noticed, is a little tight.

This is the very first shirt I ever received. You can see it’s from Pegasus software. I got this shirt 40 years ago. I am the same height — six one — as I was when I received this shirt, when I was, I think, 18.. I’m not the same weight. I was 135 pounds there then. I am not 135 pounds now, but you can see it’s a little tight, but I’m gonna wear it.

item.title: Challenges Facing Game Programmers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to continue from yesterday’s video about challenges designers face.

I want to talk about challenges programmers face. I’ve done both design and programming — about 50- Before I jump in, I apologize, my Nuka Cola shirt is in the wash.

So let’s talk about what I think is the single, most important personality attribute for programmers in the video game industry to possess. Now you may think: oh, it’s intelligence, oh, it’s a broad knowledge of programming, algorithms and data structures, or it’s specialization in one of them.

item.title: Being A Gay Game Developer
item.description:
Hi, it’s me, Tim. It is June 1st, happy pride month.

Let’s talk about being gay and being in the game industry. I got 40 years to cover four full decades. So this might be a little long. So the two long didn’t watch. The tldw is things are better. I was in the closet for the first half, not for the second half. So now let’s go through it decade by decade. So let’s start in the 80s. My first job was in 1981 at a little game company called Pegasus changed its name to cybron. I was in high school, totally in the closet. The 80s were when AIDS became a big thing. The news was constantly feeding stories about if you’re gay you’re going to die, and that scared teenager Tim luckily, the.

item.title: My First Professional Game
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the first professional game that I ever worked on.

I had fun yesterday talking about how to get a job in the industry and I said how I got my job. But this is the first game I worked on that shipped and it wasn’t why I got hired at a company in 1981.. And that, by the way, was I was still in high school. My first purchase of parachute pants was still a couple years in the future and I wish I was kidding. I basically worked at this company doing tools, for artists, because the Atari 800 had really good graphics modes and that’s why I was hired. But a couple years after I started they decided to make a bridge game — Bridge, the card game.

item.title: How to Get a Job in the Game Industry
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to answer the most asked question I ever get, not just on this channel but at conventions, by email when people visit the studios I worked at friends, family.

The number one asked question: how do I get a job in the game industry? Often with a sub question: how did you get your job? Implying that I’ll just do that. I’m going to answer the second question first because it’s fun. Then I’m going to answer the first question with tough love. Some of you won’t like the answer. Okay, so the first question or the second question I’m gonna, which I answer first, is: how did I get a job? Kind of goes back to wow, 1976 I think.

item.title: Semi-Retirement
item.description:
Hi, it’s me, Tim.

I’ve gotten some really good feedback on the site, on this channel. The people at Reddit and Discord, I guess, discovered this Channel and posted links, so getting a lot more questions than I expected this early on. They’re really good questions. Um, there’s a lot of stuff I want to address in future videos, including how I got started in the industry.

Um, oh. What inspires me in my designs, why I left Fallout 2 and mid-development? Those are all future videos, but I thought I’d clear something up, because I’m getting a lot of questions that specifically have to do with either obsidian or Microsoft, and so I just want to clear it up. I have not been a city and employee since June of 2020, uh, when I moved up to Seattle. I do work for obsidian as a contractor. I also work for another company as a contractor for them. In both cases, I’m doing design, advice and review. I also work for a third company giving them both Tech and design advice.
